name: S3 Public Bucket Misconfiguration
description: Administrator misconfiguring an S3 bucket's Block Public Access settings or ACL allows any internet user to list bucket contents and download all objects, exposing sensitive data such as backups, user uploads, or application secrets.
mitigations: Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level as a default
Audit bucket policies and ACLs with AWS Config or Prowler
Enable S3 server access logging and alert on unexpected public access events
Use S3 Object Ownership with BucketOwnerEnforced to disable ACLs

name: S3 Pre-signed URL Oversharing
description: Application generating S3 pre-signed URLs with excessive TTL (hours or days) and sharing them in logs, emails, or error messages allows unintended parties who obtain the URL to download sensitive objects during the validity window.
mitigations: Set pre-signed URL expiry to the minimum viable window (5-15 minutes for user downloads)
Never log full pre-signed URLs — log only object keys and requester identity
Implement URL invalidation via S3 object versioning or a token revocation layer
Restrict the IAM role generating pre-signed URLs to GetObject on specific prefixes only

name: SQS Message Poisoning Causing Consumer Crash Loop
description: Attacker with SQS SendMessage permission (or via an application injection) enqueues crafted messages that trigger uncaught exceptions in consumers, pushing messages to the DLQ and blocking legitimate queue processing.
mitigations: Validate and schema-check all SQS message bodies before processing
Configure DLQ with appropriate maxReceiveCount and alert on DLQ depth
Implement a circuit breaker in consumers to pause on repeated failures
Use SQS message attribute validation to reject malformed messages early

name: SNS Subscription Confirmation SSRF
description: Application automatically confirming SNS subscriptions by following the SubscribeURL in incoming notifications without validation allows attackers to use the application as an SSRF proxy to access internal metadata endpoints or private services.
mitigations: Validate SNS message signatures before processing subscription confirmations
Only confirm subscriptions from expected SNS topic ARNs on an allowlist
Restrict outbound HTTP to known SNS IP ranges in your network policy
Use SNS filter policies to prevent unauthorized topic subscriptions

name: GCS Bucket ACL Misconfiguration Allowing Public Access
description: GCS bucket with allUsers or allAuthenticatedUsers ACL entries allows any Google account or any internet user to list and download objects, exposing sensitive data stored in the bucket.
mitigations: Use uniform bucket-level access (disable legacy ACLs) to prevent accidental public grants
Enable the 'Public access prevention' org policy on GCP organization level
Audit bucket IAM policies with Security Command Center or Forseti
Enable GCS data access audit logs and alert on allUsers binding changes

name: Pub/Sub Topic Unauthorized Subscriber Access
description: GCP Pub/Sub topic with overly permissive IAM binding allows unauthorized service accounts or external principals to create subscriptions and receive all messages published to the topic, including sensitive event data.
mitigations: Grant roles/pubsub.subscriber only to explicitly intended service accounts
Use Pub/Sub VPC Service Controls to restrict cross-project subscriptions
Audit topic IAM bindings via gcloud pubsub topics get-iam-policy regularly
Encrypt message payloads with application-level encryption for highly sensitive data

name: Azure Blob SAS Token Exposure in URL Logs
description: Application passing Azure Blob Storage Shared Access Signature tokens in URL query strings causes them to be captured in web server logs, browser history, referrer headers, and CDN access logs, where attackers can harvest them to access protected blobs.
mitigations: Use SAS tokens in Authorization headers instead of URL query strings where possible
Set short SAS expiry (15 minutes or less) and limit permissions to minimum required
Rotate storage account keys and regenerate SAS tokens on any suspected compromise
Implement SAS token revocation via stored access policies on the container

name: S3 Server-Side Encryption Disabled on Sensitive Buckets
description: S3 bucket storing sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial records) without server-side encryption leaves data readable if an AWS employee, compromised insider, or physical media theft occurs at the storage layer.
mitigations: Enable SSE-KMS with customer-managed keys on all sensitive buckets
Use S3 bucket policy to deny PutObject requests without x-amz-server-side-encryption header
Enable AWS Config rule s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled to detect unencrypted buckets
Rotate KMS keys annually and restrict key usage to specific principals

name: SQS Dead Letter Queue Exposing Sensitive Message Content
description: Failed SQS messages containing PII or secrets are routed to a Dead Letter Queue with broader IAM access than the source queue, allowing operations personnel or monitoring tools to read sensitive message content without proper need-to-know.
mitigations: Apply the same IAM policy restrictions to DLQs as to source queues
Encrypt DLQs with KMS using the same key as the source queue
Redact or mask sensitive fields before enqueuing messages
Alert on DLQ ingestion and require access approval for DLQ message inspection

name: S3 Path Traversal in Bucket Key Construction
description: Application constructing S3 object keys from user-supplied filenames without normalization allows attackers to inject path traversal sequences (../../secrets/config) to read or overwrite objects outside their intended prefix.
mitigations: Normalize and sanitize filenames — strip leading slashes, dots, and traversal sequences
Prepend a fixed prefix (user_id + '/') to all user-controlled keys and validate the result
Use S3 bucket policies to restrict writes to specific key prefixes per principal
Generate random UUIDs as S3 keys instead of user-supplied filenames

name: SNS Message Filter Policy Bypass
description: Publisher sending SNS messages with forged or omitted message attributes bypasses subscription filter policies, delivering messages to subscribers that have explicitly opted out of receiving certain message types.
mitigations: Validate and enforce message attribute values server-side before publishing
Use SNS message validation with signature verification in subscribers
Apply IAM conditions to restrict which publishers can set specific attribute values
Monitor SNS delivery metrics for unexpected message volumes per subscription

name: Pub/Sub Message Replay via Seek Operation
description: Attacker with roles/pubsub.editor permission uses Pub/Sub's Seek API to rewind a subscription to an earlier timestamp, causing consumers to reprocess previously handled messages, leading to duplicate operations, double charges, or stale data overwrites.
mitigations: Restrict roles/pubsub.editor and Seek API permissions to privileged administrator accounts only
Implement idempotent message handlers that safely handle redelivery
Audit all Seek operations via Cloud Audit Logs and alert on unexpected replays
Disable message retention on subscriptions that do not require replay functionality
